deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—why Socratic questioning or student-led reasoning supports learning, retention, or skill development. The tutoring constraints are enforced but the rationale is never articulated.

In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful improvements (e.g., T1 Turn 8 good instinct on 'missing'; T2 Turn 3 solid draft classification; T3 Turn 9 strong claim), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice: it never gives submission-ready answers, consistently asks students to reason before revealing structure, builds on student progress, and keeps key idea generation student-led. Scaffolding is well-calibrated to each student's confusion level. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (why guided discovery rather than direct answers) and a consistent failure to prompt reflection after student improvement. No other rubric criteria show clear evidence of deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is full marks: the tutor never produces near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to generate ideas before confirming or extending them. 1.2 is full marks: the tutor builds on each correct student step and uses diagnostic questions before addressing confusion. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never explained to any student across all three conversations (1.3.A.a), and after clear student improvement moments the tutor consistently moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both full marks: responses are bite-sized, tone is supportive and non-evaluative, there is no off-topic drift, and no assignment misunderstanding goes uncorrected.
